title: Keyword and Semantic Search
text:
Search is everywhere
Every day we search using

🔎 Google
🛒 Amazon
🎵 Spotify
🎬 Netflix
🤖 ChatGPT

But how does a computer decide which result should appear first?

title: Keyword and Semantic Search
text:
Activity
Imagine you’re shopping online.


You search for
“headphones for studying”

Products

Gaming Headset 🎮
Sony WH-1000XM6 Noise-Cancelling Headphones 🎧
Bluetooth Speaker 🔈
Study Desk


Which product should appear first?

title: Keyword and Semantic Search
text:
Method 1: Keyword Search
Keyword search looks for the same words that appear in your query.


Query

headphones for studying


Products

Gaming Headset
Sony Noise-Cancelling Headphones
Bluetooth Speaker
Study Desk

title: Keyword and Semantic Search
text:
Activity
Suppose we use keyword search. Which products would be matched?

Bose QuietComfort Noise-Cancelling Headphones 🎧
Sony WH-1000XM6 Wireless Headphones 🎧
Desk for Studying 📚
Headphones Carrying Case 🎒
Homework Planner 📅

Which ones are actually useful?

title: Keyword and Semantic Search
text:
When keyword search struggles



You search for…
Product contains…




sneakers
running shoes


couch
sofa


physician
doctor


puppy
dog



The meanings are similar but the words are different.

title: Keyword and Semantic Search
text:
Method 2: Semantic Search
Instead of matching words semantic search tries to match meaning.
Examples

puppy ≈ dog


physician ≈ doctor


couch ≈ sofa


bicycle ≈ bike

title: Keyword and Semantic Search
text:
Example


Documents: 


['My puppy loves chasing tennis balls.',
 'My dog enjoys long walks in the park.',
 'Cats sleep for most of the day.',
 'The kitten climbed the tree.',
 'I baked chocolate chip cookies yesterday.',
 'This recipe makes delicious brownies.',
 'Vancouver received heavy rainfall today.',
 'It might rain tomorrow.',
 'Taylor Swift released a new album.',
 'I listened to some great music.',
 'I rode my bicycle to school.',
 'My bike has a flat tire.',
 'The physician recommended more exercise.',
 'The doctor prescribed antibiotics.',
 'Penguins cannot fly.']

title: Keyword and Semantic Search
text:
Keyword search vs. semantic search

compare_search("kitten")


QUERY: 'kitten'

Keyword search results
4. The kitten climbed the tree.  (score = 2.36)

Semantic search results
4. The kitten climbed the tree.  (score = 0.57)
3. Cats sleep for most of the day.  (score = 0.38)
1. My puppy loves chasing tennis balls.  (score = 0.25)
9. Taylor Swift released a new album.  (score = 0.23)
15. Penguins cannot fly.  (score = 0.19)

title: Keyword and Semantic Search
text:
Keyword search vs. semantic search

compare_search("doctor")


QUERY: 'doctor'

Keyword search results
14. The doctor prescribed antibiotics.  (score = 2.58)

Semantic search results
14. The doctor prescribed antibiotics.  (score = 0.58)
13. The physician recommended more exercise.  (score = 0.38)
11. I rode my bicycle to school.  (score = 0.16)
10. I listened to some great music.  (score = 0.15)
9. Taylor Swift released a new album.  (score = 0.13)

title: Keyword and Semantic Search
text:
Keyword search vs. semantic search

compare_search("bike")


QUERY: 'bike'

Keyword search results
12. My bike has a flat tire.  (score = 2.17)

Semantic search results
11. I rode my bicycle to school.  (score = 0.58)
12. My bike has a flat tire.  (score = 0.45)
14. The doctor prescribed antibiotics.  (score = 0.15)
2. My dog enjoys long walks in the park.  (score = 0.15)
5. I baked chocolate chip cookies yesterday.  (score = 0.15)

title: Keyword and Semantic Search
text:
Keyword search vs. semantic search

compare_search("storm")


QUERY: 'storm'

Keyword search results
No keyword matches.

Semantic search results
8. It might rain tomorrow.  (score = 0.32)
7. Vancouver received heavy rainfall today.  (score = 0.24)
15. Penguins cannot fly.  (score = 0.13)
14. The doctor prescribed antibiotics.  (score = 0.13)
9. Taylor Swift released a new album.  (score = 0.13)

title: Keyword and Semantic Search
text:
Keyword search vs. semantic search

compare_search("songs")


QUERY: 'songs'

Keyword search results
No keyword matches.

Semantic search results
10. I listened to some great music.  (score = 0.45)
9. Taylor Swift released a new album.  (score = 0.37)
8. It might rain tomorrow.  (score = 0.12)
1. My puppy loves chasing tennis balls.  (score = 0.10)
6. This recipe makes delicious brownies.  (score = 0.09)

title: Keyword and Semantic Search
section: How can a computer understand meaning?
text:
How can a computer understand meaning?

Modern AI converts words into numbers called embeddings.
Words with similar meanings end up close together.

cat 🐱 is close to kitten but far away from car

The computer compares these numbers instead of comparing words.

title: Keyword and Semantic Search
text:
Explore embeddings

https://projector.tensorflow.org/
https://word-embeddings.wbkolleg.unibe.ch/

title: Keyword and Semantic Search
text:
Activity
Which search method would you choose?

“WH-1000XM5”
“good headphones for airplanes”
“a beautiful purple dress for parties”
“Macbook Pro M5”

title: Keyword and Semantic Search
text:
Hybrid Search
Modern search systems combine keyword search and semantic search. This is called Hybrid Search.


Keyword search is great for

product names
model numbers
IDs


Semantic search is great for

descriptions
synonyms
intent
meaning

title: Keyword and Semantic Search
text:
Where is this used?
Almost every modern search system uses hybrid search.

Google Search
Amazon
Spotify
Netflix
YouTube
ChatGPT
AI assistants

title: Keyword and Semantic Search
text:
Takeaways

Keyword search looks for matching words.
Semantic search looks for matching meaning.
Modern AI systems combine both approaches.

The goal isn’t just to find the same words. It’s to find the most useful information.

title: Introduction to AI
text:
Original vs AI?
Which cats do you think are AI-generated?

title: Introduction to AI
text:
Original vs AI?
How did you decide?

title: Introduction to AI
section: Which of these do you think use AI?
text:
Which of these do you think use AI?

Google’s spam detector 📧
Autocomplete on your phone 📱
Face recognition 🔓
Voice assistants 🎤
Calculator ➕
Netflix recommendations 🍿
Alarm Clock ⏰

title: Introduction to AI
section: Which of these do you think use AI?
text:
Which of these do you think use AI?

✅ Google’s spam detector 📧
✅ Autocomplete on your phone 📱
✅ Face recognition 🔓
✅ Voice assistants 🎤
❌ Calculator ➕
✅ Netflix recommendations 🍿
❌ Alarm Clock ⏰

What makes these different?

title: Introduction to AI
text:
AI systems make predictions or decisions when there isn’t a simple set of rules. For example:

Spam detector \(\rightarrow\) Is this spam?
Autocomplete \(\rightarrow\) What word comes next?
Face recognition \(\rightarrow\) Is this the right person?
Netflix \(\rightarrow\) What movie might you like?

Whereas:

Calculator \(\rightarrow\) follows mathematical rules
Stopwatch \(\rightarrow\) counts time

title: Introduction to AI
text:
What are AI, ML, DL?


Artificial Intelligence (AI): Helps computers make predictions or decisions when there isn’t a simple set of rules.  Examples: Deep Blue, early spell checkers

Machine Learning (ML): Learning patterns from data instead of writing rules. Example: Spam filtering in Gmail

Deep Learning (DL): Using neural networks to learn complex patterns from data. Examples: ChatGPT, Face recognition in your phone

title: Introduction to AI
text:
Let’s walk through an example

Have you used search in Google Photos? You can search for “cat” and it will retrieve photos from your libraries containing cats.
This can be done using image classification.

title: Introduction to AI
text:
Image classification

Imagine we want a system that can tell cats and foxes apart.
How might we do this with traditional programming? With ML?

title: Introduction to AI
text:
Traditional programming: example





You hard-code rules. If all of the following satisfy, it’s a fox.

pointed face ✅
red fur ✅
narrow eyes ✅


This works for normal cases, but what if there are exceptions

title: Introduction to AI
text:
ML approach: example





We don’t tell the model the exact rule. Instead, we give it many labeled images, and it learns probabilistic patterns across multiple features, not rigid rules.

If fur is red \(\rightarrow\) 90% chance of Fox.

title: Introduction to AI
text:
DL approach: example





A neural network automatically learns which features to look at (edges \(\rightarrow\) textures \(\rightarrow\) objects).
No need to even specify face shape or fur colour. It learns relevant features on its own.

title: Introduction to AI
text:
Would you solve this with AI?



Problem
AI?




17 × 24



Sort a list of numbers



Is this email spam?



Addressing mental health issues



Translate this sentence



Recommend a movie



Predict tomorrow’s weather

title: Introduction to AI
text: Therapists using ChatGPT secretly 😔

title: Introduction to AI
section: When is AI a good choice?
text:
When is AI a good choice?
AI isn’t “better computing”; it’s appropriate for a different class of problems. It works well when…

✅ There are lots of examples or data to learn from.
✅ The problem involves finding complex patterns or making predictions.
✅ There isn’t a simple set of rules that always gives the right answer.

title: Introduction to AI
section: When do we still need humans?
text:
When do we still need humans?
Humans are essential when a problem requires…

🧠 Common sense and deep understanding of the situation.
❤️ Empathy, ethics, or caring for people.
⚖️ Making important decisions where mistakes have serious consequences.
…

AI can sometimes assist people, but humans should make the important decisions when empathy, trust, and responsibility matter.

title: Introduction to AI
text:
What should you remember?

AI is good at finding patterns.
AI is useful when there isn’t a perfect rule for solving a problem.
AI can help people. But people are still responsible for important decisions.

title: Welcome
text:
Welcome
Welcome everyone to the “GIRLsmarts workshop” on Introduction to AI and LLMs.

When 🕘 1:00 pm to 2:50 pm on July 4th, 2026
Where? 📍 ICICS X250 UBC, 2366 Main Mall, Vancouver, BC V6T 1Z1



This workshop aims to provide an accessible introduction to Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Large Language Models (LLMs).

title: Welcome to the workshop!
text: Focus on the breath!

title: Welcome to the workshop!
text:
Quick warm-up
Pair up with someone nearby.
Question: Where have you seen AI in real life?
Examples could include:

phone cameras
recommendations
autocomplete
…

title: Welcome to the workshop!
text:
What are we doing today?
We are going to open up the black box a little. By the end, you should be able to explain:

what people mean by AI, machine learning, and deep learning
why predicting the next word can be powerful


I’m attending this workshop because I want to learn about ___.


why search based on meaning can beat search based only on words


If I search for kitten, should I find something about cats?

title: Welcome to the workshop!
text:
The big idea
AI systems are not magic. They often work by learning patterns from examples!

title: Welcome to the workshop!
text:
Recommendations
Imagine that you want to recommend a movie to Pat. Which movie would you recommend?

title: Welcome to the workshop!
text:
Spam detection
If you’re given this data.



Email
Spam?




🎉 CONGRATULATIONS! YOU WON $1000!
✓


Your package has shipped
✗


Assignment due tomorrow
✗


YOU WON A FREE PRIZE 🎁!
✓


Dinner tonight?
✗


Can we meet at 3 PM?
✗



How would YOU build a spam detector?

title: Welcome to the workshop!
text:
All these systems are learned from examples.

Some learned patterns in images.
Some learned patterns in people’s preferences.
Some learned patterns in language.

The methods become more sophisticated, but the core idea stays the same: AI learns patterns from data.

title: Welcome to the workshop!
text:
Workshop map
We’ll explore three questions:

What is AI?
Language Models: How does your phone know what word you’ll type next?
Search: Why does Google understand your question even if the exact words don’t appear?

title: Welcome to the workshop!
text:
How we will work
This is a hands-on workshop. You will be asked to:

talk to people nearby
test what works and what fails
ask questions when something feels unclear

title: Welcome to the workshop!
text:
A useful workshop rule
When an AI system gives an answer, ask:

What pattern might it be using?

Then ask:

When might that pattern fail?

title: Welcome to the workshop!
text:
Source of the website
These slides and activities were made with Quarto.
Source: https://github.com/kvarada/GIRLsmarts-workshop

title: Language Models
section: Every day you interact with language AI
text:
Every day you interact with language AI

💬 ChatGPT answers questions
📱 Your phone finishes your sentences
📧 Gmail suggests replies
🌍 Google Translate translates languages
🔎 Google understands your searches

How do computers do this? 🤔

title: Language Models
text: Language can be ambiguous

title: Language Models
text:
Ambiguous news headlines

PROSTITUTES APPEAL TO POPE


KICKING BABY CONSIDERED TO BE HEALTHY


MILK DRINKERS ARE TURNING TO POWDER

Why are these funny?

title: Language Models
text:
Humans solve these problems effortlessly because we use

context
common sense
world knowledge

Computers don’t naturally have these abilities.

title: Language Models
text:
What is Natural Language Processing (NLP)?
Natural Language Processing (NLP) is the field of AI that focuses on enabling computers to understand, interpret, and generate human language.

title: Language Models
text:
A surprising idea
Instead of teaching computers grammar …
What if we simply taught them to predict the next word? Could that really work?

title: Language Models
text:
Activity: Become a language model
Guess the next word.



Prompt
Your prediction




Penut butter and …



Too much …



My dog at my …



Happy birthday …



The capital of Canada is …




There isn’t always one correct next word!!

title: Language Models
text:
Context matters
Which word comes next?

I went to the bank to deposit some ______

vs.

I sat on the bank of the ______


Same word. Different meanings.
Language models use context.

title: Language Models
text:
Language models assign probabilities
Instead of predicting one answer…
they estimate many possibilities.

Peanut butter and




Next word
Probability




jelly
55%


banana
25%


honey
10%


cookies
5%


…
…



The highest probability usually becomes the prediction.

title: Language Models
text:
Why does this work?
If a computer becomes really good at predicting the next word, it has learned

grammar
spelling
common phrases
facts
some reasoning patterns

without being explicitly programmed.

title: Language Models
text:
From prediction to applications
Good next-word prediction enables

✍️ Text generation
🌍 Translation
📝 Summarization
💬 Chatbots
🔎 Semantic search

Modern LLMs like ChatGPT build on this same idea.

title: Language Models
text:
For example

P(I have read this book) > P(eye have red this book) or
P(book | read this) > P(book | red this)

title: Language Models
text:
Activity: Build a Language Model
We need 10–15 volunteers.


Group 1: Full-Context Storytellers

Each volunteer receives the entire sequence generated so far.
Predict the next word.
Pass the updated sequence to the next person.
The last volunteer reads the final sentence.


Group 2: One-Word Memory Storytellers

Each volunteer receives only the previous word.
Predict the next word.
Pass only your predicted word to the next person.
Collectively generated the final sentence.

title: Language Models
text:
Reflection

Which story was easier to continue?
Which story made more sense?
What information did you use to predict the next word?
What happened when you had very little context?

Key takeaway:
The more context we have, the better we can predict what comes next.

title: Language Models
text:
Long-distance dependencies
How much context and other knowlege do you need to predict the correct next word here?

I am studying law at the University of British Columbia in Vancouver because I want to become a …


I am studying medicine at the University of British Columbia because I want to become a …

title: Language Models
text:
Long-distance dependencies

I am studying law at the University of British Columbia in Vancouver because I want to become a …



ChatGPT

lawyer ⭐
judge
prosecutor
attorney


Limited memory

good person
student
teacher
better version of myself


More context \(\rightarrow\) Better predictions.

title: Language Models
text:
Large Language Models

Modern language models like GPT-5 can use up to about 300,000 previous words of context!
This is why modern language models are so powerful.
Instead of looking at just one previous word, they can use information from hundreds of thousands of previous words to understand the conversation and generate much more coherent responses.

title: Summary and conclusion
text:
Reflect on what we covered
Discuss the following questions with your neighbour.

What is AI? Give one example where you would use AI and one where you would not.
What is a language model? Why are modern language models so powerful?
Why can searching by meaning work better than searching by exact words?

title: Summary and conclusion
text:
Final takeaway

AI is not magic. It learns patterns from data.
AI is a powerful tool but not the right tool for every problem.
The key skill is knowing when to use AI, when to question it, and when humans need to stay involved.
